text.title: Story Mapping Process
text.summary: Five-step mapping process: frame the journey, build backbone, add tasks, identify walking skeleton, slice releases. Each step has concrete outputs and a verification question.

rule.statement: Before writing any activities, define: who is the user, what is their big goal, and where does the journey start and end.
rule.rationale: Without a journey frame the backbone drifts into product-internal activities instead of user-facing ones.

rule.statement: Write activities as verb phrases on cards, arrange left-to-right in journey order, then verify the sequence by reading it aloud as a user narrative.
rule.rationale: The read-aloud test catches activities in the wrong order and reveals missing steps before you add tasks.

rule.statement: For each activity: list what the user does there, place tasks in the activity column, arrange vertically by importance (top = most important). Do not write tasks before the backbone is complete.
rule.rationale: Writing tasks before the backbone produces task lists, not a story map. The backbone provides the grouping context that gives vertical ordering its meaning.

rule.statement: Draw a line separating the walking skeleton (one task per activity, end-to-end flow) from all remaining tasks. The skeleton must not skip any backbone activity.
rule.rationale: Skipping an activity in the skeleton means the user cannot complete the journey — the skeleton is broken by definition.

rule.statement: For each release: define the value it delivers, draw a horizontal line, name the release goal, and verify the slice spans all backbone activities.
rule.rationale: Naming the release goal forces the team to ask "what can a user do after this release that they could not do before?" If the answer is unclear, the slice is not ready.

rule.statement: Do not run backbone, task, and release-slicing in one pass. Run as three sequential steps; quality drops sharply when combined.
rule.rationale: Each step requires a different thinking mode. Combined passes produce noise-rich maps with undisciplined priority ordering.
